**My Dearest Eliza,**

**I write to you with a heavy heart, for five long years have passed since we last spoke, and the world feels ever more distant. The sea, that ancient, unyielding thing, has once again begun to call to me, and tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself questioning whether I shall see another dawn.**

**I have not been the same man since you left, and though I try to keep the lighthouse burning, I fear that the winds may be too fierce, the waves too wild. I have lit the lamp, but I have not been able to keep the light from the sea. I fear that the light may go out, and with it, the storm may claim me.**

**I have not written to you before, for I have not had the strength, nor the will. But I must now, for the storm is coming, and I fear for my life. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write to thee with a heavy heart and a trembling hand, for the ink has long dried and the quill is but a mere shadow of its former self. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the world has turned many times upon its axis. I have kept the lighthouse burning, as I have always done, though the sea has grown wilder, and the winds have howled with a fury that shakes the very bones. Tonight, a storm of such ferocity and wrath that even the old sea gods would tremble in their shells. I fear I may not survive it, and if I do not, I fear I may not be able to see thee again. Yet, I write this letter, for I have a duty to fulfill, and a promise to keep. Though I am but a humble keeper of a humble light, I have kept the flame alive, and I have kept the promise alive. I pray that thee may forgive the silence, for I have been a fool, and have kept thee waiting. I pray that thee may find solace in the memory of our past, and may find peace in the love that once was. I shall not write more, for I fear the storm may take me before I can. I hope that thee may one day find this letter, and that it may bring thee comfort. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I penitently sit by the window, the rain has been relentless all day, and the wind howls like a banshee's lament. The sea, once so calm and blue, now roars with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this old tower. I have not heard from you in five years, and I fear that the storm that approaches now may be the end of our tale. 

I have kept the light burning, as I always have, for the safety of those who sail these seas. But I fear that the light may not be enough to guide them through this tempest. I have prepared the lantern, as well as the oil, the ropes, and the tools, but I fear the sea may be too wild for any vessel to brave. 

I have thought much of you these past years, and I have been tormented by the silence of your absence. I have written to you often, but you have never answered. I have been a fool, I suppose, to think that you would rather have your father's company than the company of a stranger. I have no bitterness, only sorrow, for I have loved you with all my heart, and I have always wished for your happiness and your peace. 

But now, as the storm gathers strength, I fear that I may not see you again. I have no wish to live, but I have the wish to die, if
